channel.title: Awesome Search KG
channel.description: Last 10 notes on Awesome Search KG

item.title: Doug Turnbull
item.description: Doug Turnbull Co-founder of OpenSource Connections (OSC); co-author of Relevant Search (O’Reilly).

item.title: Exploring Hierarchical Navigable Small World
item.description: Exploring Hierarchical Navigable Small World A hands-on deep dive into the HNSW algorithm written by two Vespa summer interns who built visualization and analysis tools during their exploration.

item.title: Late Interaction Models: How to Scale & Optimize in Elasticsearch
item.description: Late Interaction Models: How to Scale & Optimize in Elasticsearch Part 2 of Elastic’s ColPali series.

item.title: Elastic
item.description: Elastic What They Build Elastic makes Elasticsearch — the dominant open-source distributed search engine built on Apache Lucene.

item.title: Vespa
item.description: Vespa What They Build Vespa is an open-source big data serving engine originally developed by Yahoo and now maintained as an independent open-source project (vespa.ai).

item.title: ACORN-1
item.description: ACORN-1 Definition ACORN-1 is an HNSW-based approximate nearest neighbor algorithm designed to handle aggressive metadata filtering better than standard HNSW.

item.title: ColPali
item.description: ColPali Definition ColPali is a visual document retrieval model that applies Late Interaction (ColBERT-style MaxSim scoring) to document page images.

item.title: HNSW
item.description: HNSW — Hierarchical Navigable Small World Graph-based approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) index. Malkov & Yashunin, 2018 (arXiv:1603.09320).

item.title: Intent Drift
item.description: Intent Drift Definition Intent drift is the gradual or sudden shift in what users expect from a query, even when the query text stays the same.

item.title: Late Interaction
item.description: Late Interaction Definition Late interaction is a retrieval architecture where query and document representations are computed independently (like Bi-Encoder) but interact at the token level during scoring (unlike single-vector bi-encoders).
